stories.story: Since Iran's Islamic Revolution in 1979, women have been barred from attending most sports events involving men. The situation appears set to improve after a top Iranian sports official said the ban will be lifted for some events. Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi said a plan to allow "women and families" to enter sports stadiums will come into effect in the next year, according to state-run media. It isn't clear exactly which games women will be able to attend. According to the state-run Press TV, Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi said the restrictions would be lifted for indoor sports events.
key_facts: Women barred from most men's sports events since 1979 Islamic Revolution
Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi announced a plan for women and families to enter stadiums in the next year
Restrictions will be lifted for indoor sports events according to Press TV
explicit_unknowns: exactly which games women will be able to attend

stories.story: According to the state-run Press TV, Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi said the restrictions would be lifted for indoor sports events. Press TV cited Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi as saying rules won't change for all matches because some sports are mainly related to men and "families are not interested in attending" them. Iranian authorities imposed the ban on women attending men's sports events after the revolution, deeming mixed crowds un-Islamic. During the ensuing decades, crowds at soccer games, Iran's most popular sport, have been all male. Iranian women were briefly permitted to attend volleyball matches under President Mohammad Khatami, but the ban was reinstated in 2005 after Mahmoud Ahmadinejad came to power.
key_facts: Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi stated some sports are for men and families are not interested in them
The ban was based on the belief that mixed crowds are un-Islamic
Soccer crowds in Iran have been all male for decades
President Mohammad Khatami briefly allowed women at volleyball matches
The ban was reinstated in 2005 under Mahmoud Ahmadinejad

stories.story: Iranian women were briefly permitted to attend volleyball matches under President Mohammad Khatami, but the ban was reinstated in 2005 after Mahmoud Ahmadinejad came to power. The Iranian government has come under pressure from international sports officials over the restrictions. FIFA President Sepp Blatter called on Iran to end its "intolerable" ban on women attending soccer matches, and the 2019 Asian Cup was awarded to the United Arab Emirates. The ban on women attending matches was widely seen as a major impediment to Iran's chances of securing the 2019 Asian Cup. The ban came under the spotlight at the Asian Cup in Australia earlier this year, when thousands of female Iranian fans watched their soccer team without restriction.
key_facts: President Mohammad Khatami and Mahmoud Ahmadinejad had opposing policies on volleyball attendance
FIFA President Sepp Blatter called the ban on women attending soccer matches "intolerable"
The 2019 Asian Cup was awarded to the United Arab Emirates instead of Iran
The ban was seen as a major impediment to Iran securing the 2019 Asian Cup
Female Iranian fans watched their team without restriction at the Asian Cup in Australia

stories.story: The ban came under the spotlight at the Asian Cup in Australia earlier this year, when thousands of female Iranian fans watched their soccer team without restriction. During the match against Iraq, activists unfurled a banner showing the face of Ghoncheh Ghavami, a British-Iranian woman detained in Iran last year while trying to watch a volleyball match. Iranian officials denied Ghoncheh Ghavami was arrested for attending the volleyball game, citing "anti-Iran activities," though the news agency Reuters reported she was recently pardoned by the Court of Appeal. CNN's Annie Ramos contributed to this report.
key_facts: Female Iranian fans watched their team without restriction at the Asian Cup in Australia
Activists used a banner of Ghoncheh Ghavami during a match against Iraq
Ghoncheh Ghavami is a British-Iranian woman detained last year
Iranian officials claimed Ghoncheh Ghavami was detained for "anti-Iran activities"
Reuters reported that the Court of Appeal pardoned Ghoncheh Ghavami
CNN's Annie Ramos contributed to the report
